title: Corneal amyloid staining after trypan blue in Descemet's membrane endothelial keratoplasty.
journal: American journal of ophthalmology case reports
abstract: This case report seeks to highlight impressive photographs of amyloid staining with trypan blue that persisted after DMEK surgery with 3 years follow-up. This has only been reported in the literature twice previously, and may have contributed to visual symptoms in the early post-operative period of this patient. We report a case of an 82-year-old patient with concurrent Fuchs' corneal endothelial dystrophy and lattice corneal dystrophy who suffered permanent trypan blue staining of the amyloid deposits after descemet's membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) surgery. Trypan blue staining of amyloid deposits in the corneal stroma is permanent, with research suggesting potential stimulation of progression or recurrence of amlyoid deposition. This is relevant for all anterior segment surgeons using trypan blue in the setting of primary (eg. lattice corneal dystrophy) or secondary amyloid deposition (eg. polymorphous amyloid degeneration, chronic inflammation, systemic disease). Trypan blue staining should be limited or alternative surgical options such as descemet's stripping automated endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK) considered.
full_text:
Corneal amyloid staining after trypan blue in Descemet's membrane endothelial keratoplasty☆

Purpose

This case report seeks to highlight impressive photographs of amyloid staining with trypan blue that persisted after DMEK surgery with 3 years follow-up. This has only been reported in the literature twice previously, and may have contributed to visual symptoms in the early post-operative period of this patient.

Observations

We report a case of an 82-year-old patient with concurrent Fuchs' corneal endothelial dystrophy and lattice corneal dystrophy who suffered permanent trypan blue staining of the amyloid deposits after descemet's membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) surgery.

Conclusions and importance

Trypan blue staining of amyloid deposits in the corneal stroma is permanent, with research suggesting potential stimulation of progression or recurrence of amlyoid deposition. This is relevant for all anterior segment surgeons using trypan blue in the setting of primary (eg. lattice corneal dystrophy) or secondary amyloid deposition (eg. polymorphous amyloid degeneration, chronic inflammation, systemic disease). Trypan blue staining should be limited or alternative surgical options such as descemet's stripping automated endothelial keratoplasty (DSAEK) considered.

Introduction

Amyloid deposits can occur with corneal dystrophy (eg. Lattice corneal dystrophy) or as a secondary consequence (polymorphous deposits, chronic inflammation, systemic disease), with the latter being associated with polymorphic amyloid degeneration, a non-heritable and non-progressive amyloid deposition in elderly patients, which can sometimes be misinterpreted as corneal guttata.

Trypan blue dye has been safely used for anterior segment surgeries for some time. There are case reports of inadvertent corneal stromal staining with trypan blue, including from direct injection. In one case, the staining resolved with conservative treatment in 7 weeks.

There are only two case series in the current literature on staining of corneal stromal amyloid deposits using trypan blue during anterior segment surgery. Of these cases, one case was during penetrating keratoplasty, one during descemet's membrane endothelial keratoplasty after failed PK for lattice dystrophy, and the other cases were during deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty or cataract extraction. In all of these cases, the staining was persistent. Whilst not visually significant in any case, previous case reports have suggested a stimulatory effect on the progression of amyloidosis.

We report a case of an 82 year-old patient with concurrent Fuchs’ corneal endothelial dystrophy and lattice corneal dystrophy who suffered permanent trypan blue staining of the amyloid deposits after DMEK surgery.

Case report

An eighty-two year old male with known concurrent lattice corneal dystrophy and Fuchs’ corneal endothelial dystrophy presented with reduced vision and diurnal fluctuation in the right eye on a background of poor vision secondary to full-thickness macular hole. His best-corrected Snellen visual acuity at the time was 6/60. He had previously undergone pars plana vitrectomy and internal limiting membrane peel combined with cataract extraction and insertion of intraocular lens for brunescent cataract and full-thickness macular hole. His previous best-corrected visual acuity in this eye was known to be 6/18 after the combined surgery. His contralateral left eye has long-standing poor vision secondary to refractive ambylopia. He was otherwise well with no known systemic medical illnesses. Endothelial Specular Microscopy showed reduced endothelial cell count (835) and polymegathism. Confocal microscopy was not available.

DMEK graft post vision blue staining prior to insertion.

Fig. 1

He underwent successful Descemet's Membrane Endothelial Keratoplasty (DMEK) with 20% Sulfur Hexafluoride (SF6) intraocular gas tamponade. The DMEK corneal graft was prestripped by the local eye bank 1 day prior. Stripping was completed during surgery and subsequently stained with Trypan Blue (VisionBlue, DORC, Netherlands) for 3 minutes. Trypan Blue was not used intraoperatively to assist in descemetorhexis or graft unfolding. An inferior peripheral laser iridotomy was created pre-operatively to prevent pupil block. Injection of the graft with a glass pipette (DMEK injector, Geuder, Germany) and unfolding of the graft proceeded uneventfully. The graft showed a modest amount of staining prior to insertion (Fig. 1). The anterior chamber was filled to around 80% with 20% SF6 (EasyGas, Fluoron GmbH, Germany).

Postoperative day 1, showing early lattice staining, condensed centrally.

Fig. 2

Postoperative day 5, showing DMEK graft in situ with lattice staining and gas bubble clearing.

Fig. 3

At 3 years follow-up, amyloid lattice lines remain stained with trypan blue.

Fig. 4

On post-operative day 1, the graft appeared attached with a 80% gas fill. Staining of the lattice lines were apparent (Fig. 2). Unfortunately, as the graft cleared, the staining did not dissipate. At day 5 post-operatively, the DMEK graft can be seen attached with ongoing lattice staining and clearing gas fill (Fig. 3). The patient's best corrected visual acuity reached 6/24 at two weeks, although the patient complained of glare. At 3 years follow-up, the patient's visual acuity had returned to baseline at 6/18, but the stained lattice lines were still apparent but less intense (Fig. 4).

Discussion

Descemet's membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) is being increasingly utilised in corneal transplant surgery. The use of trypan blue (TB) staining is required to ensure optimal visualisation of graft orientation and unfolding. Trypan blue will delineate collagen type IV-rich membranes, and permanent discolouration is avoided by dilution with balanced salt solution. There are only a small number of case reports in the current literature regarding TB staining of lattice lines in cataract and corneal transplant surgery, mostly around the graft-host junction after penetrating keratoplasty (PK) or deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK) surgery. There is one previous report after DMEK surgery for a failed PK with recurrent lattice dystrophy. Ours is the first case to our knowledge after primary DMEK.

It is possible that reduced TB staining time of the DMEK corneal tissue could result in lower chance of lattice line staining, but this has not been investigated. Tissue stained for between 1 and 3 minutes may be effective enough for uncomplicated DMEK surgery, however lighter stains will make the surgery more difficult. In this case, all of the subsequent trypan staining of lattice was derived from that bound to the graft prior to insertion. In this case, it is unclear if the lattice staining post-operatively may have been visually significant, as the patient was suffering from increased glare and photophobia in the early post-operative phase. This had settled during subsequent follow-up visits, and the staining can be seen to have lightened at 3 years follow-up, Fig. 4.

There are other reports of TB staining of the corneal stroma during inadvertent injection, but these resolve spontaneously. Trypan blue is known to stain basement membranes. Trypan blue staining of a DMEK corneal graft therefore occurs via staining of the posterior corneal Descemet's basement membrane. It is possible amyloid deposition within a basement membrane component could be staining in this case, however amyloid deposits in corneal amyloid disorders, such as lattice dystrophy, are found scattered throughout the corneal stroma. The permanent staining duration indicates a strong interaction between TB and amyloid proteins directly. Aggregates of amyloid deposited onto cell membranes interact with TB, enhancing the deposition of trypan blue onto the cell membrane, rather than increasing intracellular uptake of the dye. Since TB is unable to cross intact basement membranes, the removal of host Descemet's membrane during DMEK surgery provides direct access of TB to amyloid deposits in the posterior stroma. Despite the permanent amyloid staining not being visually significant in previous studies, animal models referenced in the case series by Farooq et al., 2014, suggest a stimulatory effect on the progression of amyloidosis.

In conclusion, the risk of permanent amyloid staining in patients with lattice or other amyloid deposition conditions should be recognised by anterior segment surgeons using intraoperative trypan blue. The authors suggest minimising the use of trypan blue during DMEK preparation and surgery, or performing DSAEK surgery as an alternative.

Claims of Priority: After conducting a literature review on 7th October 2023 utilizing PubMed using the keywords trypan blue, staining, amyloid, DMEK, we found no previous reports of lattice amyloid deposits stained during primary DMEK (other cases were after other anterior segment surgery).
